body_txt: Gaslighting is deliberately trying to drive someone mad by altering their environment without their knowledge, then denying it. In a more general sense, it's denying someone's perceptions of reality in order to cause confusion, anguish, and paranoia. You move their things, transmit noises into their room when no one else is there, change little details about your dress behind their back, and so on. Or you start acting drastically differently than you usually do . When they confront you about it, you claim to have no idea what they’re talking about and feign concern for their memory and mental health. Soon, they are convinced that they're hearing voices, seeing dead people, hallucinating, or whatever. The victim can become so convinced that they're going insane that they actually go insane . The main difference between this and being a Manipulative Bastard is that they are more about lying and targeting someone's weakness, whereas to gaslight someone is to manufacture changes into their life to make them easier to manipulate. The name comes from the 1938 play Gaslight (later adapted into two separate films of the same name), where a woman's abusive husband manipulates her into believing she is going insane by moving some objects around and claiming that she was the one responsible for hiding them. note Ironically, the dimming of the "gaslights" referred by the movie's title are not part of the husband's manipulation, making this an Unbuilt Trope . The husband is looking to steal the wife's valuables in the attic, so by turning on the attic lights, he's inadvertently redirecting the gas away from the house's main lights, causing them to dim in front of the wife, who is unsure if she's imagining it. The husband doesn't actually understand how the gaslights work as noted in the original play where the Detective has to explain to the abusive husband how turning on a set of gaslights would cause another set to lose some of their illumination. In real life, this is a common tactic employed by the abuser in abusive relationships , especially those that revolve around an imbalanced power dynamic, including domestic abuse and school/workplace bullying. The goal for the abuser is to trick the victim into doubting their own sound judgement and perception at every turn, which usually serves a two-fold purpose. Firstly to make the victim susceptible to the idea that if the abuser commits a violent or other criminal/transgressional act on them, it didn't actually happen, just like the other things the abuser denied happened. Secondly, if the victim can be persuaded that they are not of sound mind and only the abuser is capable of seeing the truth, they can be fooled into depending on the abuser to tell them what's real and what's not, keeping them in proximity for the abuser to commit more abusive acts against them. In short, the abuser is able to both cover their crimes and gain the victim's captive loyalty by telling them a convincing lie enough times that their word becomes "the truth". For similar reasons, the technique has also been employed for purposes of political repression by the Secret Police agencies of authoritarian/totalitarian regimes, both on a mass scale and towards individual targets deemed threats. As in fiction , this method of manipulation often leaves deep psychological scars that require extensive efforts to correct, including therapy and in some cases, even medication. The abuse victims suffer, and the destabilisation of their psyche often plays a role in later manifestations of mental illnesses such as complex post-traumatic stress disorder (C- PTSD ). "Gaslighting" has undergone a certain amount of lexical devaluation. Rather than meaning "subtly influencing another's perception of reality to cause self-doubt," it is also often used to refer to subtle deception in general, or even simply meaning "to lie", or more specifically, "to lie brazenly and in the face of obvious evidence to the contrary , even when there's no existing trust to betray". At worst, one party in a conflict recalls events differently and accuses the other of "gaslighting" them even if no such malice is intended. See also Why Did You Make Me Hit You? , Driven to Madness , Break Them With Lies , Paranoia Gambit , Living Lie , and 2 + Torture = 5 .
